g.data-narration: Welcome to Compiler Design! Let's explore how compilers translate high-level code into machine-executable programs.
A compiler is a translator that converts human-readable source code into machine-executable code while performing error detection and optimization.
g.text: A translator that converts high-level programming language code
into machine-executable code while checking for errors
and optimizing output for better performance.

g.data-narration: A compiler translates the entire program at once creating an executable, while an interpreter translates and executes line by line.
g.text: Faster execution | Reports all errors | More memory
Slower execution | Stops at first error | Less memory

g.data-narration: The compiler works through six phases: Lexical, Syntax, Semantic analysis (Front End) plus Code Generation phases (Back End).
Phase 1 - Lexical Analysis breaks source code into tokens, the smallest meaningful units like keywords, identifiers, and operators.
g.text: Token: (name, value) | Lexeme: actual characters | Pattern: token description

g.data-narration: Phase 2 - Syntax Analysis verifies tokens follow grammatical rules and builds a parse tree representing the code structure.
g.text: Grammar: Production rules | Syntax Errors: Rule violations

g.data-narration: Phase 3 - Semantic Analysis checks meaning beyond syntax, performing type checking, scope resolution, and type coercion.
g.text: Input: Syntax Tree → Output: Annotated Tree

g.data-narration: Phase 4 - Intermediate Code Generation creates machine-independent three-address code, a simple representation for optimization.
g.text: Input: Annotated Tree → Output: Three-Address Code
Common IR: Three-address code, Quadruples, Triples, AST

g.data-narration: Phase 5 - Code Optimization improves code efficiency through machine-independent and machine-dependent techniques like constant folding.
g.text: Input: Intermediate Code → Output: Optimized Code

g.data-narration: Phase 6 - Code Generation produces the final target machine code or assembly code from optimized intermediate representation.
g.text: Input: Optimized IR → Output: Machine/Assembly Code
MOV R1, a | MUL R1, b | MOV t1, R1

g.data-narration: The Symbol Table stores information about all identifiers like variables and functions throughout all compilation phases.
g.text: Data structure storing identifier information used by all phases

g.data-narration: Compilers detect errors at different phases: lexical, syntax, semantic, and logical errors each require different recovery strategies.
g.text: Error detection and recovery across compilation phases
Recovery: Panic mode, Phrase level, Error productions, Global correction

g.data-narration: Different compiler types serve specific purposes: single-pass, multi-pass, cross-compilers, and JIT compilers each have unique use cases.
A C program compilation involves preprocessing, compilation to assembly, assembly to object code, and linking to create the executable.
g.text: Linking combines object files and libraries to create the final executable.

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: Six phases transform source to target code, Front End handles analysis, Back End handles synthesis and code generation.
In summary, compilers translate high-level code through six phases while detecting errors and optimizing for efficient machine code generation.
g.text: Lexical → Syntax → Semantic → IR Gen → Optimization → Code Generation
A compiler is essential for translating high-level code to machine-executable form.
Understanding these phases helps in writing efficient code and building language tools.
Refer: Dragon Book | Modern Compiler Implementation | Engineering a Compiler
